findings.justification: Nonce verification and all input reads use $_REQUEST instead of $_POST, which allows GET-based requests to carry these parameters and can weaken CSRF protection in certain server/proxy configurations.

An authenticated admin can supply arbitrary strings via $_REQUEST['capabilities'] to control the capability scanner scope; sanitize_key does not restrict to a whitelist of valid WordPress capabilities, so an attacker with admin access could target arbitrary user groups.

scan_recommendations() automatically revokes capabilities and destroys all sessions for detected users as a side effect of generating the report HTML, meaning any code path that calls it (including the AJAX endpoint when completed=true) triggers irreversible account changes without a separate confirmation step.

The $content string built in scan_recommendations() is stored to the database via update_option('um_secure_scan_result_content', $content) and contains concatenated raw HTML using esc_url/admin_url but without wp_kses applied to the full accumulated string before storage.

Active plugin status is determined via apply_filters('active_plugins', ...) which can be overridden by any installed plugin, potentially causing the security scanner to give false assurances about security plugins being active.

get_option('um_secure_scanned_details', $susp_accounts) passes an array of WP_User objects as the default value, so if the option is absent $option is an array of user objects and the subsequent $option['scanned_caps'] access will produce a PHP warning and undefined behavior rather than a clean fallback.
